teams.name: Mexico
teams.style: Pragmatic 4-3-3 with a compact mid-block, direct pacey wingers and strong set pieces
keyPlayers: Raúl Jiménez (Fulham, ST — scored in the opener)
teams.form: Unbeaten through 2026 and reigning Gold Cup/Nations League champions; opened the World Cup with a 2-0 win over South Africa at the Azteca.
teams.outlook: Altitude, home noise and Jiménez's form make them group favorites (Opta 48%), but creativity vs low blocks is thin and César Montes is suspended for the South Korea game.

teams.name: South Korea
teams.style: Flexible 4-2-3-1/3-4-3 built around Kim Min-jae, organized mid-block with rapid vertical transitions
keyPlayers: Son Heung-min (LAFC, FW, captain — 9 goals in 15 MLS games in 2026)
Lee Kang-in (PSG, AM — assisted the equalizer vs Czechia)
Hwang In-beom (Feyenoord, CM — goal and assist vs Czechia)
teams.form: Only unbeaten AFC qualifier (6W 4D); came from behind to beat the Czech Republic 2-1 on matchday one.
teams.outlook: Elite spine and proven resilience, but heavy reliance on Kim Min-jae's fitness and Son's 33-year-old legs, with finishing efficiency a nagging worry.

teams.name: Czech Republic
teams.style: Pragmatic, physical low-block 3-4-2-1; the best set-piece team in UEFA qualifying, weaponizing Coufal's long throws
keyPlayers: Patrik Schick (Bayer Leverkusen, ST — 9 goals in his last 8 club games)
Ladislav Krejčí (Wolves, CB — scored vs Korea)
teams.form: Reached a first World Cup since 2006 via playoff shootout wins over Ireland and Denmark; won both June friendlies before the 2-1 loss to South Korea.
teams.outlook: Schick's red-hot form and dead-ball menace give a real third-place-or-better path, but open-play creativity is thin and the back line lacks pace.

teams.name: South Africa
teams.style: Disciplined 4-3-3 low-mid block with quick counters, built on a cohesive Mamelodi Sundowns core
keyPlayers: Teboho Mokoena (Mamelodi Sundowns, CM, set-piece specialist)
teams.form: Won CAF Group C, but exited AFCON in the last 16, drew Nicaragua 0-0, then lost 2-0 to Mexico with two red cards.
teams.outlook: Organized, cohesive and well-goalkept, but the attack is blunt and the suspensions of Sithole and Zwane gut them for the must-win Czechia game.

groups.groupReading: The high altars of Mexico anoint their hosts, who glide through on thin air and thick noise while Korea's swift spirits follow close behind. The Czech towers fall short of the top two yet may slip through the third-place gate; the southern lions roared bravely but depart with their discipline in tatters.

matches.reading: The orb glowed over the Azteca before a ball was kicked... thin air at 2,240 metres and eighty thousand voices were always going to smother a Bafana side that creates little against elite blocks. Quiñones' strike and Jiménez's header shimmer in the glass — though three red mists swirled that no seer could love.
matches.keyFactor: Altitude and Azteca crowd overwhelm South Africa

matches.reading: The mist parts to show Coufal's long throw thundering in and Krejčí rising — yet the spirits favor the silkier feet. Hwang In-beom's Feyenoord form burns brightest; Korea's transitions wear down a Czech back line short of pace before the night grows old, even as Son's chances flicker and fade.
matches.keyFactor: Korea's transition pace against a slow Czech back three

matches.reading: Schick's flame — nine goals in his last eight for Leverkusen — licks at a South African block stripped of the suspended Sithole and Zwane. The bones grant Mokoena one thunderbolt from a dead ball, but the tall ones from Prague score twice in Atlanta's sealed noon heat and end Bafana's road.
matches.keyFactor: South Africa's suspensions meet Schick's hot streak

matches.reading: Two winners circle each other in Guadalajara's night and the crystal clouds, for Montes' suspension leaves a door ajar for Son's diagonal runs. Yet Jiménez's gravity, Lozano's directness and a second home cauldron tilt the glass — a narrow El Tri verdict, though a draw whispers loudly from the edges.
matches.keyFactor: Home cauldron vs Mexico's makeshift, Montes-less defense

matches.reading: By the third turning of the cards Mexico may already be crowned, yet the Azteca never sleeps and Aguirre's bench runs deep. The Czech towers rattle the thin air once from a corner, but Vega and Mora run at tiring legs all evening and the hosts finish unbeaten on their mountain.
matches.keyFactor: Mexico's depth and a second Azteca night at altitude

matches.reading: In Monterrey's glass-walled furnace the orb shows taegeuk red... Korea need little here, but Son's farewell tour does not idle. Williams' gloves delay the inevitable and Mofokeng conjures one moment of joy, yet an attack that managed fifteen goals in ten qualifiers cannot answer twice.
matches.keyFactor: Korea's attacking class vs South Africa's blunt attack

teams.name: Canada
teams.style: High-octane 4-4-2 'Maplepressing' — aggressive high press, forced turnovers and fast vertical transitions
keyPlayers: Alphonso Davies (Bayern Munich, LB, captain — back from 2025 ACL tear)
teams.form: Seven-game unbeaten run in warm-ups; opened the World Cup with a 1-1 draw vs Bosnia (Lukic corner-kick header threatened, Larin super-sub cameo).
teams.outlook: Elite athleticism, pressing and all three group games on home soil, but Davies (hamstring) misses the opener and Canada have never won a World Cup match (0-6).

teams.name: Switzerland
teams.style: Flexible, possession-oriented 4-2-3-1 flowing through Xhaka, with a miserly mid-block and fast breaks via Ndoye
keyPlayers: Granit Xhaka (Sunderland, CM, captain — record 145 caps, fourth World Cup)
teams.form: Won UEFA qualifying group unbeaten (only 2 conceded); beat Jordan 4-1 and drew Australia 1-1 in warm-ups; opened the World Cup with a 1-1 draw vs Qatar — Embolo penalty 17', Khoukhi equalizer 90+4.
teams.outlook: The group's best defense and most battle-tested spine, but conceding a last-gasp equalizer to Qatar raises questions about concentration — and the core is aging. They've stalled in second place at three straight World Cups.

teams.name: Qatar
teams.style: Spanish-school possession 4-3-3 with Afif free between the lines — open, entertaining and defensively leaky
keyPlayers: Akram Afif (Al Sadd, AM — 11 assists in qualifying)
Almoez Ali (Al Duhail, ST — AFC qualifying top scorer)
teams.form: Winless in six pre-tournament; opened the World Cup with a HISTORIC 1-1 draw vs Switzerland — Khoukhi's 90+4 equalizer earned Qatar their first-ever World Cup point in their 10th attempt.
teams.outlook: Qatar's resilience and Lopetegui's organisation earned a point no one foresaw; now Canada await at BC Place. The first point may unlock belief. Goals will still be hard to find, but a draw is no longer unthinkable.

teams.name: Bosnia and Herzegovina
teams.style: Compact 4-4-2, direct service into the Džeko–Demirović front two, with a heavy set-piece emphasis
keyPlayers: Edin Džeko (Schalke 04, ST, captain — 40 years old, record 72 international goals)
teams.form: Qualified via March playoff penalty wins over Wales and Italy; drew final warm-up 1-1 with Panama; opened the World Cup with a 1-1 draw vs Canada — Lukic's corner-kick header the standout moment, Džeko's shoulder kept him off the bench.
teams.outlook: Shootout-forged nerve was evident at BMO Field, and they're still level with Canada and Switzerland on 1 point. Džeko's shoulder fitness vs Switzerland is the swing factor between a famous qualification run and an early exit.

groups.groupReading: The glass's first vision shattered on matchday one: all four sides locked on one point apiece, Qatar having etched their name into history at the 90th minute's dying breath. The maple press and the Swiss wall remain the glass's favoured pair, but both must earn what they assumed was already written — Xhaka vs Lukic, David vs Afif's belief now alive. The final order may still read as foreseen, but only after the hardest second matchday anyone expected.

matches.reading: The maple mist rises over BMO Field even without Davies, whose hamstring keeps the captain robed at kickoff. Marsch's press will harry a Bosnia built around a 40-year-old Džeko nursing a wounded shoulder — yet the old wolf bites once against the run of the cards before David, hungry after a lean Juventus year, settles a nervous home debut.
matches.keyFactor: Canada's press and home crowd vs Džeko's aging but cunning threat

matches.reading: The glass barely fogs here: a Swiss wall that conceded twice in all of qualifying meets a Qatar side winless in six and leaking goals. Ndoye's runs and Embolo's shoulders breach the maroon line either side of the hour; Afif conjures sparks in the Bay Area sun, but no fire.
matches.keyFactor: Switzerland's elite defense against winless, leaky Qatar

matches.reading: Under SoFi's silver roof the runes counsel caution — Xhaka must turn the wheel while Akanji's back line answers questions it never faced in qualifying. Switzerland allowed Khoukhi to equalise in the 94th minute against Qatar, a lapse of concentration that Bosnia's set-piece sorcery — Lukic's corner-kick header against Canada already confirmed the danger — will punish if repeated. Džeko, still nursing the shoulder that kept him on the bench in Toronto, remains the great unknown; if he starts, the aerial menace doubles. Yet Swiss technical superiority and Kobel's gloves are sterner than what a last-gasp Qatari defensive midfielder provided — two Nati strikes on either side of the break, with Bosnia landing one from a dead ball to keep the ledger honest.
matches.keyFactor: Switzerland's concentration after the Qatar lapse vs Bosnia's proven set-piece danger — Džeko shoulder fitness the decisive swing

matches.reading: Vancouver's harbour wind carries red waves — but the orb now reads a Qatar transformed by Khoukhi's last-gasp point against Switzerland. That 90+4 equalizer is not mere fortune; it is proof that Lopetegui's organisation can survive ninety minutes of pressure and bite at the death. Canada, still searching for their first group-stage win after the BMO draw, welcome Davies's return to tip the balance from the left — yet Afif and Almoez Ali will not merely ship goals politely in BC Place's smaller confines. Two Canadian strikes are the glass's verdict, but Qatar's belief earns one back before the final whistle.
matches.keyFactor: Canada's press and Davies's return vs Qatar's newly-found World Cup resolve — no longer a foregone conclusion

matches.reading: The scales tremble and refuse to settle — both keepers of this gate likely advance, and the spirits smell prudence in the air. Kobel's gloves against Davies' lightning, Xhaka's tempo against Marsch's storm... the orb shows a single strike apiece and handshakes at the whistle.
matches.keyFactor: Both sides likely already through; a draw suits everyone

matches.reading: Seattle's grey skies suit the dragons' last stand — and if the shoulder has healed enough to start, Džeko's header at the far post is all the prophecy Bosnia need. If not, Jovo Lukic proved at BMO Field that Bosnia do not need the old wolf to threaten set pieces; the same Bosnian 4-4-2 discipline that held Canada level can contain Afif's sparks long enough. Lopetegui's men will land one moment of craft, but Bosnia's shootout-forged nerve carries them through.
matches.keyFactor: Bosnia's set-piece threat (with or without Džeko starting) vs Qatar's soft defensive center

teams.name: Brazil
teams.style: Fluid 4-2-3-1/4-3-3 with an all-out front line — Vinícius left, Raphinha right — and rotating midfield shapes
keyPlayers: Vinícius Júnior (Real Madrid, FW — 22 goals in 53 club games in 2025-26)
Raphinha (Barcelona, W — 21 goals and 7 assists in 33 matches)
Neymar (Santos, FW — calf injury doubt, fourth World Cup)
teams.form: Won four of last six pre-tournament; drew 1-1 with Morocco in the opener at MetLife — conceded Saibari's strike in the 21st minute before Vinícius equalized in the 32nd, but couldn't find a winner.
teams.outlook: Elite wingers and five-star pedigree, but the leaky defence conceded to Morocco's debutant striker and Scotland now lead Group C. Brazil must chase group leadership through games vs Haiti and Scotland — the Neymar fitness saga shadows the camp.

teams.name: Morocco
teams.style: 4-2-3-1 morphing into 4-2-2-2 to open a motorway lane for Hakimi — more expansive and possession-dominant than the Regragui era
keyPlayers: Achraf Hakimi (PSG, RB, captain — reigning African Player of the Year)
Brahim Díaz (Real Madrid, AM — AFCON 2025 Golden Boot)
teams.form: Perfect 8-for-8 in CAF qualifying, AFCON 2025 champions; drew 1-1 with Brazil in the opener — Saibari opened the scoring in the 21st minute only for Vinícius to equalize in the 32nd. Controlled large stretches but couldn't hold.
teams.outlook: World-class spine and 2022 knockout pedigree, but the new manager's system is still finding its edge. A win vs Scotland would book their place in the knockouts and potentially the group crown.

teams.name: Scotland
teams.style: Organized 4-2-3-1 with set-piece strength and big-game midfield runners; can drop to a back five against elite sides
keyPlayers: Scott McTominay (Napoli, CM — 2024-25 Serie A MVP, scored the bicycle kick that sealed qualification)
John McGinn (Aston Villa, CM — scored the winner vs Haiti)
teams.form: First World Cup since 1998; warmed up with a 4-1 win over Curaçao and 4-0 over Bolivia; OPENED THE WORLD CUP WITH A WIN — McGinn's deflected strike in the 28th minute beat Haiti 1-0, putting Scotland TOP of Group C for the first time since 1974.
teams.outlook: Scotland are TOP of Group C on 3 points — their first World Cup win since 1998. Clarke's disciplined back-five absorbed Haiti's threat comfortably; Gilmour's absence and no elite striker remain the ceiling, but history is genuinely within reach if they draw Morocco.

teams.name: Haiti
teams.style: Compact, counter-attacking underdogs — young diaspora squad (average age 24) with genuine pace in transition
keyPlayers: Duckens Nazon (ST — all-time top scorer with 44 goals)
teams.form: Won their CONCACAF qualifying group despite playing every 'home' game abroad; beat New Zealand 4-0 then lost 2-1 to Peru; lost opener 0-1 to Scotland (McGinn 28') — Pierrot's late header flashed wide.
teams.outlook: The pilgrimage is still beautiful but the road narrows: Brazil await on matchday two, and elimination in the group stage now looks the likely ending. Isidor and Bellegarde carry the spirit forward regardless.

groups.groupReading: The glass's first night burned tartan: McGinn's deflected winner sent Scotland to the top of Group C — a position no Scots side has held at a World Cup since Archie Gemmill danced in Argentina. Yet the orb still foresees the giants recovering: Brazil's firepower overwhelms Haiti, Morocco's precision edge Scotland in Foxborough, and the June 24 collision in Miami under the lights remains the chapter that will be recited for decades. Scotland fall one place short of the top but walk through the third-place gate with their heads held high; Haiti's beautiful pilgrimage ends in song.

matches.reading: The orb churns gold and crimson over MetLife — Vinícius and Raphinha arrive in the form of their lives, yet Brazil have conceded in five straight and Militão's absence gapes at the back. Hakimi's motorway lane carves one answer and Bono stands tall, but the canary firepower edges a duel the bones call nearly even.
matches.keyFactor: Brazil's elite wingers vs their own leaky, depleted backline

matches.reading: Fifty-two years of waiting fills Gillette with Haitian song, yet the tartan spirits march colder and deeper into the night. McTominay's late surge and a set-piece thunderclap appear in the glass; Isidor's pace draws gasps from the mist, but no goals.
matches.keyFactor: Scotland's set pieces and superior squad depth

matches.reading: The Tartan Army return to Foxborough as group leaders — a sentence that has never been written before — but the glass cautions against the champagne. Clarke's back five, disciplined against Haiti's direct threat, now faces Hakimi's motorway lane and Brahim Díaz's left foot, a combination several grades above Nazon and Bellegarde. Without Gilmour's calm feet Scotland must surrender the ball for long ages; one moment of Atlas precision cuts through a Scottish organisation that cannot press for ninety minutes at this level. Morocco win narrowly, and the October-like Gillette night tells Scotland they must win Miami to make history.
matches.keyFactor: Morocco's attacking class — Hakimi and Brahim Díaz — vs Scotland's organised back five without Gilmour's ball progression

matches.reading: Philadelphia's cauldron shows no mercy in the glass: the widest talent gulf in Group C, and Ancelotti's men — already stung by Scotland's early lead in this group — smell a goal-difference feast against the tournament's thinnest squad. A draw against Morocco is not the start Brazil wanted; the response here must be overwhelming. Neymar, if the calf spirits permit, writes a cameo verse, and the canary firepower pours through every gap Haiti cannot close. Four canary strikes shimmer in the crystal, though Haiti's pace on the break conjures one long-distance threat that Cavaleiro's gloves smother.
matches.keyFactor: Brazil needing goals to chase Scotland and Morocco; Haiti's thinnest squad in the field can't resist elite pressing for 90 minutes

matches.reading: Miami's golden hour hosts a Group C decider the orb never foresaw: Scotland's McGinn-led warriors, already the first Scots to lead this group since 1974, against a Brazil side chasing a crown they dropped in Foxborough. If Clarke's men need only a draw and Brazil need a win, the tactical battle becomes existential for both. McTominay's late surge may forge one moment of tartan defiance under the Florida lights — but Vinícius and Raphinha in full canary cry, running at legs that have fought through two 90-minute battles, will find the seams in a back five that cannot contain this much individual quality for an entire game. One Scottish goal writes history; two Brazilian replies end the fairytale with honour intact.
matches.keyFactor: Brazil's attacking brilliance and Miami heat vs Scotland's back-five discipline — McTominay the one tartan card that can change the night

matches.reading: Atlanta's sealed roof traps the Atlas lions' roar — a side that won all eight qualifiers against the lowest-ranked dreamers in the field. Hakimi marauds down his motorway, Brahim finishes twice, and Bono's clean sheet is etched in the glass before the second candle burns down.
matches.keyFactor: Morocco's perfect-qualifying machine vs the tournament's thinnest squad
